randomNumber:
program random_number_demo
  implicit none
  integer :: seed_size, i
  integer, allocatable :: seed(:)
  real(kind=real64) :: random_val_single
  real(kind=real64), dimension(5) :: random_val_array
  integer :: min_val, max_val, random_int

  ! Seed the generator (important to do once or when reseeding is needed)
  call random_seed(size = seed_size) ! Get required seed size
  allocate(seed(seed_size))
  ! You could fill seed with values from date_and_time or other sources
  ! For simplicity, let the system initialize it on first call (behaviour can vary)
  ! Or explicitly call random_seed() with PUT to set a seed.
  call random_seed() ! Initialize or re-initialize based on system state

  ! Generate a single random real number [0.0, 1.0)
  call random_number(random_val_single)
  write (*, '(A, F8.6)') 'Single random real [0,1): ', random_val_single

  ! Generate an array of random real numbers [0.0, 1.0)
  call random_number(random_val_array)
  write (*, '(A)') 'Array of random reals [0,1):'
  write (*, '(5F8.6)') random_val_array

  ! Generate a random integer in a range [min_val, max_val]
  min_val = 1
  max_val = 100
  call random_number(random_val_single) ! Get a real in [0,1)
  ! Scale and shift: INT( R * (MAX - MIN + 1) ) + MIN
  random_int = int( random_val_single * real(max_val - min_val + 1) ) + min_val
  ! Note: Ensure rounding/truncation behavior is as expected for the range edges.
  ! Ensure random_int doesn't exceed max_val if random_val_single is exactly 1.0 (rare).
  if (random_int > max_val) random_int = max_val

  write (*, '(A, I0, A, I0, A, I0)') 'Random integer [', min_val, '-', max_val, ']: ', random_int

  deallocate(seed)

contains

  ! Define kind constants if iso_fortran_env is not used
  integer, parameter :: real64 = kind(1.0d0)

end program random_number_demo
reverseString:
program reverse_string_demo
  implicit none
  character(len=:), allocatable :: original, reversed_str
  integer :: i, len

  original = 'Hello Fortran'
  len = len(original)

  if (allocated(reversed_str)) deallocate(reversed_str)
  allocate(character(len=len) :: reversed_str)

  ! Manual reversal character by character
  do i = 1, len
     reversed_str(i:i) = original(len - i + 1 : len - i + 1)
  end do

  write (*,'(A)') 'Original: '// trim(original)
  write (*,'(A)') 'Reversed: '// trim(reversed_str)

  if (allocated(reversed_str)) deallocate(reversed_str)

  ! Note: Standard Fortran has no intrinsic string reverse function.
  ! Some compiler extensions or libraries might provide one.
  ! This manual approach works for standard strings but doesn't handle
  ! multi-byte characters (like UTF-8) correctly if used byte-wise.
  ! For UTF-8, specific character handling logic would be needed.

end program reverse_string_demo
fibonacci:
program fibonacci_demo
  use iso_fortran_env, only: int64 ! Use 64-bit integers for larger range
  implicit none
  integer, parameter :: num_terms = 10
  integer, parameter :: large_num_terms = 93 ! Near int64 limit
  integer :: i
  integer(kind=int64) :: t1, t2, next_term

  ! Generate first 'num_terms'
  write(*, '(A, I0, A)') 'Fibonacci Sequence (first ', num_terms, ' terms):'
  t1 = 0
  t2 = 1
  do i = 1, num_terms
     if (i > 1) write(*, '(A)', advance='no') ', ' ! Comma separator after first term

     if (i == 1) then
        write(*, '(I0)', advance='no') t1
     else if (i == 2) then
        write(*, '(I0)', advance='no') t2
     else
        ! Overflow is not automatically checked/handled portably in standard Fortran.
        ! Behaviour depends on compiler flags and processor.
        ! Could add manual checks if needed: if (t2 > huge(t1) - t1) stop 'Overflow'
        next_term = t1 + t2
        write(*, '(I0)', advance='no') next_term
        t1 = t2
        t2 = next_term
     end if
  end do
  write(*,*) ! Newline

  ! Attempt 'large_num_terms' (may overflow depending on compiler/flags)
  write(*, *) ! Blank line
  write(*, '(A, I0, A)') 'Fibonacci Sequence (first ', large_num_terms, ' terms - may overflow):'
  t1 = 0
  t2 = 1
   do i = 1, large_num_terms
     if (i > 1) write(*, '(A)', advance='no') ', '
     if (i == 1) then
        write(*, '(I0)', advance='no') t1
     else if (i == 2) then
        write(*, '(I0)', advance='no') t2
     else
        next_term = t1 + t2
        write(*, '(I0)', advance='no') next_term
        t1 = t2
        t2 = next_term
     end if
  end do
  write(*,*) ! Newline

end program fibonacci_demo
factorial:
program factorial_demo
  use iso_fortran_env, only: int64 ! Use 64-bit integers
  implicit none
  integer :: n
  integer(kind=int64) :: result

  write(*,*) 'Calculating Factorials:'

  n = 5
  result = factorial(n)
  write(*,'(A, I0, A, I0)') 'Factorial of ', n, ' = ', result

  n = 0
  result = factorial(n)
  write(*,'(A, I0, A, I0)') 'Factorial of ', n, ' = ', result

  n = 20 ! Max for int64
  result = factorial(n)
  write(*,'(A, I0, A, I0)') 'Factorial of ', n, ' = ', result

  n = 21 ! Will overflow int64
  write(*,'(A, I0, A)') 'Attempting Factorial of ', n, ' (will likely overflow)...'
  result = factorial(n)
  write(*,'(A, I0, A, I0)') 'Factorial of ', n, ' = ', result, ' (Result likely incorrect)'

contains

  ! Recursive or iterative function for factorial
  function factorial(k) result(fact)
    integer, intent(in) :: k
    integer(kind=int64) :: fact
    integer :: i
    ! Assumes k >= 0. Add check if needed.
    if (k < 0) then
       write(*,*) 'Error: Factorial input must be non-negative.'
       fact = -1 ! Indicate error
       return
    end if

    fact = 1_int64 ! Initialize result as int64
    if (k > 0) then
      do i = 1, k
        ! Standard Fortran does not guarantee overflow checking.
        fact = fact * i
      end do
    end if
  end function factorial

end program factorial_demo
primeCheck:
program prime_check_demo
  use iso_fortran_env, only: int64
  implicit none
  integer(kind=int64) :: nums_to_check(*) = [0_int64, 1_int64, 2_int64, 3_int64, 4_int64, &
                                            & 17_int64, 25_int64, 29_int64, 97_int64, 100_int64, &
                                            & 104729_int64, -5_int64]
  integer :: i

  do i = 1, size(nums_to_check)
     if (is_prime(nums_to_check(i))) then
        write(*,'(I0, A)') nums_to_check(i), ' is a prime number.'
     else
        write(*,'(I0, A)') nums_to_check(i), ' is not a prime number.'
     end if
  end do

contains

  ! Function to check if a number is prime
  function is_prime(n) result(prime_status)
    use iso_fortran_env, only: int64, real64
    integer(kind=int64), intent(in) :: n
    logical :: prime_status
    integer(kind=int64) :: i, limit

    prime_status = .false. ! Assume not prime initially
    if (n <= 1_int64) return
    if (n <= 3_int64) then
       prime_status = .true.
       return
    end if
    if ( (modulo(n, 2_int64) == 0) .or. (modulo(n, 3_int64) == 0) ) return

    ! Calculate integer limit as floor(sqrt(n))
    ! Need real conversion for sqrt
    limit = int(sqrt(real(n, kind=real64)), kind=int64)

    i = 5_int64
    do while (i <= limit)
       if ( (modulo(n, i) == 0) .or. (modulo(n, i + 2_int64) == 0) ) then
          return ! Found a factor
       end if
       i = i + 6_int64
    end do

    ! If loop finishes, it's prime
    prime_status = .true.
  end function is_prime

end program prime_check_demo
readFile:
program read_file_demo
  implicit none
  character(len=80) :: line ! Buffer to read lines into
  character(len=20), parameter :: filename = 'example_fortran.txt'
  integer :: unit_number, io_status

  ! Generate an example file
  open(newunit=unit_number, file=filename, status='replace', action='write', iostat=io_status)
  if (io_status /= 0) stop 'Error creating example file!'
  write(unit_number, '(A)') 'First line from Fortran.'
  write(unit_number, '(A)') 'Second line of the example.'
  write(unit_number, '(A)') 'Third line.'
  close(unit_number)
  write(*,*) 'Created/Reset example file: ', trim(filename)
  write(*,*) ''

  ! Open the file for reading
  ! newunit gets an unused unit number automatically (F2008+)
  ! For older Fortran, assign a fixed number like unit=10
  open(newunit=unit_number, file=filename, status='old', action='read', iostat=io_status)

  ! Check if open was successful
  if (io_status /= 0) then
     write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Error opening file for reading, status: ', io_status
     stop 'File open error'
  end if

  write(*,'(A, A, A)') '--- Contents of ', trim(filename), ' ---'

  ! Read line by line until end of file
  do
     read(unit_number, '(A)', iostat=io_status) line
     ! Check for end-of-file or error
     if (io_status < 0) exit ! End of file reached (standard negative value)
     if (io_status > 0) then  ! Error occurred
        write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Error reading line, status: ', io_status
        exit ! Exit loop on error
     end if

     ! Process the line (here, just print it, trimming trailing blanks)
     write(*,'(A)') trim(line)
  end do

  write(*,*) '--- End of file ---'

  ! Close the file
  close(unit_number, iostat=io_status)
  if (io_status /= 0) then
     write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Error closing file, status: ', io_status
  end if

end program read_file_demo
writeFile:
program write_file_demo
  implicit none
  character(len=20), parameter :: filename = 'output_fortran.txt'
  integer :: unit_number, io_status, i

  ! --- Write mode (overwrite/create) ---
  write(*,'(A, A)') '--- Writing (overwrite) to: ', trim(filename)
  open(newunit=unit_number, file=filename, status='replace', action='write', iostat=io_status)

  if (io_status /= 0) stop 'Error opening file for writing!'

  ! Write some lines
  write(unit_number, '(A)', iostat=io_status) 'Line 1 written by Fortran.'
  if (io_status /= 0) stop 'Error writing line 1!'
  write(unit_number, '(A)') 'Using formatted write.'
  do i = 1, 3
     write(unit_number, '(A, I0)') 'Record number: ', i
  end do

  ! Close the file
  close(unit_number, iostat=io_status)
  if (io_status /= 0) stop 'Error closing file after write!'
  write(*,*) 'Initial content written.'


  ! --- Append mode ---
  write(*,*) ''
  write(*,'(A, A)') '--- Appending to: ', trim(filename)
  ! status='old' ensures file exists, position='append' starts writing at end
  ! access='append' is another common way (compiler dependent?)
  open(newunit=unit_number, file=filename, status='old', position='append', action='write', iostat=io_status)
  ! Alternative/Older: open(unit=11, file=filename, status='old', access='append', iostat=io_status)


  if (io_status /= 0) stop 'Error opening file for appending!'

  write(unit_number, '(A)') '--- Appended Content ---'
  write(unit_number, '(A)') 'This line was appended.'

  ! Close the file
  close(unit_number, iostat=io_status)
  if (io_status /= 0) stop 'Error closing file after append!'
  write(*,*) 'Content appended.'

  write(*,*) ''
  write(*,'(A, A)') 'Check contents of: ', trim(filename)

end program write_file_demo
fileExists:
program file_exists_demo
  implicit none
  character(len=30) :: filename
  logical :: file_exists, is_dir ! Variables to hold results
  integer :: file_size ! Example: get file size too

  write(*,*) 'Checking file existence using INQUIRE:'

  ! --- Test cases ---
  filename = 'code-snippets.php' ! Assumed to exist
  call check_existence(filename)

  filename = 'output_fortran.txt' ! Should exist if writeFile ran
  call check_existence(filename)

  filename = 'nonexistent_fortran.f90' ! Should not exist
  call check_existence(filename)

  filename = 'snippets' ! Assumed to exist (directory)
  call check_existence(filename)

contains

  subroutine check_existence(fname)
    character(len=*), intent(in) :: fname
    logical :: exists_flag, is_directory_flag, is_file_flag
    integer :: size_val, open_stat
    character(len=20) :: action_val ! To check if it's a file or directory sometimes

    write(*,'(A, A)') 'Checking: ', trim(fname)

    ! INQUIRE statement checks file properties without opening
    ! exist= tells if the file/directory exists
    inquire(file=trim(fname), exist=exists_flag)

    if (exists_flag) then
       write(*,*) '  -> Exists: .true.'

       ! Check if it's a directory (less standard, try checking action or size)
       ! 'action' might be 'READ', 'WRITE', 'READWRITE' for files, undefined/error for dirs?
       ! This is not perfectly reliable across systems/compilers for dir check.
       inquire(file=trim(fname), opened=is_directory_flag, action=action_val, size=size_val)
       ! A simpler, often effective check (though not foolproof): can we open it as a file?
       open(unit=99, file=trim(fname), status='old', action='read', iostat=open_stat)
       if (open_stat == 0) then
           is_file_flag = .true.
           close(99)
       else
           is_file_flag = .false. ! Likely a directory or permission issue
       end if

        write(*,'(A, L1)') '  -> Is likely a File?       : ', is_file_flag
        ! is_directory_flag based on 'opened' is not reliable here. Check !is_file_flag.
        write(*,'(A, L1)') '  -> Is likely a Directory?  : ', .not. is_file_flag

        ! Get file size if it's a file
        if (is_file_flag) then
            inquire(file=trim(fname), size=size_val)
             write(*,'(A, I0, A)') '  -> Size (bytes)           : ', size_val
        end if

    else
       write(*,*) '  -> Exists: .false.'
    end if

  end subroutine check_existence

end program file_exists_demo
dateTime:
program date_time_demo
  implicit none
  ! date_and_time provides values in arrays/integers
  integer, dimension(8) :: date_time_vals ! For DATE_AND_TIME
  integer :: year, month, day, zone_diff_mins, hour, minute, second, ms
  character(len=10) :: date_str ! YYYYMMDD
  character(len=11) :: time_str ! HHMMSS.sss
  character(len=6) :: zone_str  ! +/-HHMM

  ! 1. Call the intrinsic subroutine
  call date_and_time(date_str, time_str, zone_str, date_time_vals)

  ! 2. Extract values from the integer array 'date_time_vals'
  ! Index:    1     2     3      4          5     6     7        8
  ! Value: Year, Month, Day, ZoneDiffMins, Hour, Min, Second, Millisecond
  year     = date_time_vals(1)
  month    = date_time_vals(2)
  day      = date_time_vals(3)
  zone_diff_mins = date_time_vals(4) ! Minutes difference from UTC
  hour     = date_time_vals(5)
  minute   = date_time_vals(6)
  second   = date_time_vals(7)
  ms       = date_time_vals(8)

  write(*,*) '--- Current Date and Time ---'
  write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Year         : ', year
  write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Month        : ', month
  write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Day          : ', day
  write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Hour (local) : ', hour
  write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Minute (local): ', minute
  write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Second (local): ', second
  write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Milliseconds : ', ms
  write(*,'(A, I0, A)') 'Zone Diff (mins from UTC): ', zone_diff_mins, &
       & ' (Note: sign indicates local = UTC + diff)'

  ! 3. Using the character string outputs
  write(*,*) ''
  write(*,'(A, A)') 'Date String (YYYYMMDD) : ', date_str
  write(*,'(A, A)') 'Time String (HHMMSS.sss): ', time_str
  write(*,'(A, A)') 'Zone String (+/-HHMM)  : ', zone_str

  ! 4. Manual Formatting Example
  ! Write to an internal character variable (internal file)
  character(len=30) :: formatted_string
  write(formatted_string, '(I4.4,"-",I2.2,"-",I2.2," ",I2.2,":",I2.2,":",I2.2)') &
       year, month, day, hour, minute, second
  write(*,*) ''
  write(*,'(A, A)') 'Formatted (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS): ', trim(formatted_string)

  ! 5. Getting Unix Epoch Time (seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC)
  ! Standard Fortran doesn't have a direct intrinsic. Often requires system calls
  ! or compiler-specific extensions. Some compilers provide SYSTEM_CLOCK.
  ! integer(kind=int64) :: epoch_seconds
  ! call system_clock(count=epoch_seconds) ! Count might be ticks, need count_rate
  ! write(*,*) 'Epoch seconds might require SYSTEM_CLOCK or system calls.'

end program date_time_demo
maxInArray:
program max_in_array_demo
  implicit none
  integer, parameter :: n = 9
  integer, dimension(n) :: numbers = [10, 5, 42, 17, 8, 99, 32, -5, 0]
  integer :: max_val_intrinsic
  integer :: max_val_manual, i

  write(*,'(A)') 'Array:'
  write(*,'(9I5)') numbers ! Print the array content

  ! Method 1: Using intrinsic function MAXVAL (preferred)
  max_val_intrinsic = maxval(numbers)
  write(*,*) ''
  write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Maximum value (using MAXVAL intrinsic): ', max_val_intrinsic

  ! Method 2: Manual loop
  if (n <= 0) then
     write(*,*) 'Manual check: Array is empty!'
     max_val_manual = -huge(max_val_manual) ! Indicate error/empty with smallest integer
  else
     max_val_manual = numbers(1) ! Assume first is max
     do i = 2, n
        if (numbers(i) > max_val_manual) then
           max_val_manual = numbers(i)
        end if
     end do
      write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Maximum value (manual loop):          ', max_val_manual
  end if

  ! Example with empty array (intrinsic handles this, returns negative HUGE value)
  integer, dimension(0) :: empty_array
  write(*,*) ''
  write(*,'(A, I0)') 'Max of empty array (using MAXVAL): ', maxval(empty_array)

  ! Manual check for empty array
   if (size(empty_array) <= 0) then
      write(*,*) 'Manual check confirmed: Array is empty!'
   end if

end program max_in_array_demo
